title: Introduction to std::array
summary_md:
## Introduction to std::array

You already know `std::vector`, which can grow and shrink at runtime. When the number of elements is known at compile time and will never change, `std::array` (from `<array>`) is the better choice. It has a fixed size baked into its type, uses stack memory instead of heap allocation, and carries zero overhead compared to a raw C-style array.

### Declaring and Initializing

`std::array` is a class template that takes two template arguments: the element type and the number of elements. You initialize it with a brace-enclosed list, just like `std::vector`:

```cpp
#include <array>
#include <iostream>

int main()
{
    std::array<int, 4> scores{ 90, 85, 72, 98 };

    scores[2] = 75;                  // modify third element
    std::cout << scores[0] << '\n';  // 90
    std::cout << scores[2] << '\n';  // 75
}
```

If you provide fewer initializers than the array size, the remaining elements are value-initialized (zero for numeric types). An empty initializer list `{}` zeros the entire array.

### Accessing Elements

`operator[]` accesses an element by index, starting from `0`, exactly like `std::vector`. No bounds checking is performed, so an out-of-range index is undefined behavior. The `.at()` member function does check bounds and throws `std::out_of_range` on a bad index, but it is slower.

### Key Differences from std::vector

- The size is part of the type: `std::array<int, 3>` and `std::array<int, 4>` are completely different types.
- No `push_back`, `pop_back`, or `resize` -- the element count is fixed forever.
- Allocated on the stack, so no heap allocation cost.
- Supports `constexpr` -- an entire `std::array` can be created and used at compile time.

### When to Prefer std::array

Use `std::array` whenever the element count is a compile-time constant: small lookup tables, fixed-size coordinate tuples, RGB color values, or configuration parameters that never change. Use `std::vector` when the size is determined at runtime or the collection needs to grow.

exercises.title: Sum and Average of Fixed Scores
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Declare a `std::array` of integers, read values into it, then compute and print the sum and average.

## Input
- A single line containing exactly 5 space-separated integers.

## Output
- Line 1: the sum of all 5 integers
- Line 2: the average as a `double`, printed with exactly 1 decimal place

## Requirements
1. Declare a `std::array<int, 5>` to hold the scores.
2. Use a range-based `for` loop to read each element from `std::cin`.
3. Use a second range-based `for` loop to compute the sum.
4. Compute the average as a `double` and print it with `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(1)`.
5. Include `<array>`, `<iostream>`, and `<iomanip>`.

## Notes
- The average of `{10, 20, 30, 40, 50}` is `30.0`.

exercises.title: Count Elements Above Threshold
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Read a fixed-size array of temperatures and a threshold value, then report how many temperatures exceed the threshold and print them.

## Input
- Line 1: exactly 6 space-separated integers (the temperatures)
- Line 2: a single integer (the threshold)

## Output
- Line 1: the count of temperatures strictly greater than the threshold
- Remaining lines: each temperature that exceeds the threshold, printed one per line, in their original order

If no temperature exceeds the threshold, print only `0`.

## Requirements
1. Declare a `std::array<int, 6>` for the temperatures.
2. Use a range-based `for` loop to read the values.
3. Use a range-based `for` loop to count how many exceed the threshold. Store the count in a variable of type `std::size_t`.
4. Print the count first, then iterate again and print each exceeding temperature on its own line.
5. Do not use `std::vector` or any dynamic container.

## Notes
- This exercise combines `std::array` (this lesson) with range-based `for` loops (Ch 16) and basic comparison operators (Ch 6).

test_cases.label: Hidden -- all above threshold with negatives
